methodology: Static review of architecture, data flow, persistence, and concurrency boundaries
Targeted inspection of shared training corpus model code used by the LumeLabel target
Build verification with /usr/bin/xcodebuild -project Ilumionate.xcodeproj -scheme LumeLabel -destination 'platform=macOS' build

build_verification.primary_failure: Access control prevents split view extensions from compiling

findings.title: Target does not compile because split-file extensions reference private state and fileprivate helpers
findings.symptom: The main target fails to build because `LabelingDetailView+Subviews.swift` reaches into `private` state and methods declared in a different file.
findings.impact: This is a release-blocking defect. Nothing else in the target matters until the app can compile.
evidence: xcodebuild reports `'seek' is inaccessible due to 'fileprivate' protection level` and repeated `private protection level` errors for `currentTime`, `viewStart`, `viewEnd`, `lastMagnification`, `isPlaying`, and related helpers.
Because the view was intentionally split across files, `private` and `fileprivate` are the wrong encapsulation boundary.
findings.codesnippet:
LumeLabel/LabelingDetailView.swift: `@State private var currentTime: TimeInterval = 0`, `@State private var viewStart: Double = 0`, `func seek(to time: TimeInterval)`, `func togglePlayback()`
LumeLabel/LabelingDetailView+Subviews.swift: uses `currentTime`, `viewStart`, `viewEnd`, `lastMagnification`, `seek(...)`, `togglePlayback()`, `zoomIn()`, `zoomOut()`, `zoomFit()`

recommended_fix.approach: Use `private` only for members consumed inside the same file. Promote cross-file view state/helpers to internal or move them into a private nested view model/type owned by `LabelingDetailView`.
steps: Decide whether the split-file pattern is staying.
If yes, remove `private`/file-scoped restrictions from members needed by sibling extensions.
Prefer moving timeline/playback state into a dedicated observable model so extension files consume a single coherent API instead of many raw fields.

findings.title: Detail screen edits a value snapshot instead of shared corpus state, creating stale and divergent data flow
findings.symptom: Selection and editing are driven by copied `LabeledFile` values rather than a stable identity or binding into the source of truth.
findings.impact: Background updates, re-saves, or external mutations can leave the sidebar, detail view, and persisted JSON out of sync. This is especially risky because the app is supposed to produce trusted ground-truth labels.
evidence: ContentView stores `@State private var selectedFile: LabeledFile?` and passes that value into `LabelingDetailView(file: file)`.
LabelingDetailView then copies it again into `@State var file: LabeledFile`.
TrainingCorpusManager.save(_:)` replaces entries inside `labeledFiles`, but neither `selectedFile` nor the detail view is rebound to that updated source.
findings.codesnippet:
ContentView.swift: `@State private var selectedFile: LabeledFile?`
LabelingDetailView.swift: `@State var file: LabeledFile`

recommended_fix.approach: Select by stable identity, not by value snapshot.
steps: Store `selectedFileID: LabeledFile.ID?` in `ContentView`.
Resolve the current file from `corpus.labeledFiles` on demand.
Pass a binding or an editor model that saves against the canonical ID.
Avoid duplicating file state in both the parent and the detail view unless there is explicit draft-management logic.

findings.title: Import pipeline launches an unstructured task that can overwrite newer edits with stale file snapshots
findings.symptom: Duration loading happens in a fire-and-forget `Task` that captures a mutable copy of the imported file, then later saves that stale copy back to disk.
findings.impact: If the user starts labeling immediately after import, the later duration write can clobber newer phases, notes, or metadata with the old imported snapshot.
evidence: The code does `var file = try corpus.importAudio(from: url)` and later `file.audioDuration = ...; try? corpus.save(file)` inside `Task { ... }`.
There is no cancellation, version check, or reload-by-ID before the delayed save.
findings.codesnippet: CorpusSidebarView.swift: `var file = try corpus.importAudio(from: url)` followed by `Task { ... file.audioDuration = CMTimeGetSeconds(duration); try? corpus.save(file) }`

recommended_fix.approach: Keep import enrichment structured and identity-based.
steps: Move metadata extraction into `TrainingCorpusManager.importAudio` or into a dedicated import service.
If async enrichment must remain separate, reload the latest file by ID before saving.
Track import tasks and cancel them when the view disappears or a duplicate import supersedes them.

findings.title: Corpus persistence runs synchronous file I/O on the MainActor
findings.symptom: The entire corpus manager is `@MainActor`, but it performs blocking directory scans, JSON decoding, encoding, copies, writes, and deletes synchronously.
findings.impact: Large corpora or big audio imports will stall the UI thread and make labeling interactions feel unreliable or frozen.
evidence: `loadAll()` uses `contentsOfDirectory`, `Data(contentsOf:)`, and decode loops on the main actor.
`save(_:)`, `importAudio(from:)`, and `delete(_:)` all perform blocking filesystem work directly.
This manager is injected as the app-wide observable source of truth, so UI code calls these paths frequently.
findings.codesnippet: TrainingCorpusManager.swift: `@MainActor @Observable final class TrainingCorpusManager { ... }` with synchronous `Data(contentsOf:)`, `data.write(to:, options: .atomic)`, `copyItem`, and `removeItem` calls

recommended_fix.approach: Separate UI state from persistence execution.
steps: Move disk work into a non-main actor repository or isolated storage actor.
Keep only published state mutations on the main actor.
Expose async save/load/import APIs and await them from the UI.

findings.title: Import identity is based on filename, which causes collisions, accidental data reuse, and unsafe deletes
findings.symptom: Imported audio is stored under `audioDirectory/<original filename>` and delete removes by filename as well.
findings.impact: Two different source files with the same basename can collide. Later imports may silently reuse the wrong audio, and deleting one label can remove audio still referenced by another label.
evidence: `let filename = sourceURL.lastPathComponent` and `let destURL = audioDirectory.appending(path: filename)`
Copy is skipped if the destination already exists, regardless of file contents.
Delete removes `audioDirectory.appending(path: file.audioFilename)` with no reference counting or ownership model.
findings.codesnippet: TrainingCorpusManager.swift: `if !FileManager.default.fileExists(atPath: destURL.path()) { try FileManager.default.copyItem(at: sourceURL, to: destURL) }` and later `try? FileManager.default.removeItem(at: audioURL)`

recommended_fix.approach: Persist audio with collision-safe storage identities.
steps: Use a generated storage filename such as `<uuid>.<ext>` or content-addressed filenames.
Store the original display name separately from the storage path.
Before delete, verify whether other corpus records still reference the same stored asset.

findings.title: Phase editing permits invalid training annotations with overlaps, gaps, and out-of-order boundaries
findings.symptom: The label editor mutates raw arrays directly but never validates phase invariants before saving.
findings.impact: Ground-truth data can become logically inconsistent, which undermines any downstream analyzer training or evaluation built on this corpus.
evidence: `markPhaseStart` only sets the previous end time to `currentTime` and appends a new phase ending at full duration.
Removing a middle phase in `phaseRow` simply removes the element and leaves surrounding ranges untouched.
There is no normalization pass to enforce sorted, contiguous, non-overlapping, bounded segments.
findings.codesnippet:
LabelingDetailView.swift: `file.phases[file.phases.count - 1].endTime = currentTime` then append a new phase
LabelingDetailView+Subviews.swift: `file.phases.remove(at: index)`

recommended_fix.approach: Treat phase ranges as a validated domain model, not an unguarded array.
steps: Introduce a phase-editing reducer/service that centralizes insert, remove, split, merge, and normalize rules.
Validate before every save: sorted by start time, start < end, within duration, no overlaps, no negative lengths.
Surface validation failures in the UI instead of persisting corrupted labels.

findings.title: Errors are broadly suppressed with `try?`, causing silent corruption and impossible-to-diagnose failures
findings.symptom: The app routinely discards important failures instead of surfacing them or logging enough context to recover.
findings.impact: Persistence, decoding, audio loading, and hashing failures can look like 'nothing happened' to the user while the corpus drifts into an incomplete state.
evidence: `player = try? AVAudioPlayer(contentsOf: url)` hides playback setup failures.
`try? corpus.save(file)` is used in both label save and async import enrichment paths.
`loadAll()` silently drops unreadable or undecodable JSON files via `compactMap` plus `try?`.
`ensureDirectories()` and delete operations also swallow failures.
findings.codesnippet: Examples: `player = try? AVAudioPlayer(contentsOf: url)`, `try? corpus.save(file)`, `guard let data = try? Data(contentsOf: url) else { return nil }`, `return try? decoder.decode(LabeledFile.self, from: data)`

recommended_fix.approach: Make storage and playback failures explicit.
steps: Return typed errors from persistence APIs.
Show actionable alerts for user-facing failures and log detailed diagnostics for developer-facing failures.
Preserve failed JSON filenames and decode errors during corpus load instead of dropping them silently.

findings.title: Automated tests are placeholder scaffolds and do not protect the corpus editor’s critical behaviors
findings.symptom: The target has generated template tests only; none of the corpus invariants, import flows, or editor behaviors are verified.
findings.impact: Regressions in label integrity, import correctness, file deletion, and playback/editor interaction can ship undetected.
evidence: Unit tests contain only the default example placeholder.
UI tests only launch the app and collect a screenshot.
This is especially risky because the app’s output is supposed to become training truth data.
findings.codesnippet:
LumeLabelTests.swift: `@Test func example() async throws { }`
LumeLabelUITests.swift: launches the app without assertions

recommended_fix.approach: Add tests around the data model and repository before broadening UI coverage.
steps: Unit-test phase normalization and validation rules.
Unit-test import collision handling, delete semantics, and failed decode recovery.
Add at least one UI test for importing, marking phases, saving, reopening, and verifying persisted state.

findings.title: Drag-and-drop import bypasses security-scoped resource access on macOS
findings.symptom: Dropped file URLs are imported directly, but only file-importer URLs receive `startAccessingSecurityScopedResource()`.
findings.impact: Sandboxed builds can fail unpredictably when importing dropped files outside the app container, even though the same files succeed through the file importer sheet.
evidence: The `.fileImporter` flow explicitly starts and stops security-scoped access.
The `.dropDestination(for: URL.self)` flow loops over URLs and calls `importURL(url)` without acquiring access first.
findings.codesnippet: CorpusSidebarView.swift: `.dropDestination(for: URL.self) { urls, _ in for url in urls { importURL(url) } return !urls.isEmpty }`

recommended_fix.approach: Normalize all inbound file URLs through a single access wrapper.
steps: Create one helper that conditionally starts/stops security-scoped access before import.
Use that helper from both the file importer callback and drag-and-drop handler.
Surface a clear import error when access cannot be acquired.

findings.title: Audio identity hash reads only the first 64 KB, which is too weak for reliable corpus deduplication
findings.symptom: The stored SHA-256 is computed from only the first 64 KB of the audio file.
findings.impact: Different files with identical headers or common intros can hash the same, making any future deduplication or audit tooling unreliable.
evidence: The implementation comment explicitly states it hashes only the first 64 KB.
The code reads `handle.read(upToCount: 64 * 1024)` and hashes only that partial chunk.

recommended_fix.approach: Use a full-file digest if the hash is intended to be identity-bearing.
steps: Either stream the entire file through SHA-256 or rename the field to make it explicit that it is a prefix hash.
If full-file hashing is too expensive during import, compute it asynchronously and persist both a fast provisional fingerprint and a final stable digest.

recommended_remediation_order: Fix the access-control build blocker in LabelingDetailView split files so the target compiles.
Refactor selection and editing around stable file identity instead of copied `LabeledFile` values.
Move corpus persistence and import work off the main actor and make async saves structured.
Redesign stored-audio identity to avoid filename collisions and unsafe deletes.
Add validation around phase boundaries before allowing save.
Replace silent `try?` paths with surfaced errors and diagnostics.
Backstop the corpus editor with unit tests and one end-to-end UI flow.
